evidence: We also utilize and intend to expand our use of automation, robotics and machine learning in many of our products... and we leverage generative artificial intelligence in our business processes.
Trialing such technology can take an extended period of time, with little to no returns in the short or medium terms.
We leverage generative artificial intelligence in our business processes.
We also utilize and intend to expand our use of automation, robotics and machine learning in many of our products, including consumer-facing features.
We have been involved in trialing new and evolving technology, but doing so may require significant investments of capital.

Uncertainty in the legal and regulatory regime relating to artificial intelligence may require significant resources to modify and maintain business practices to comply with international laws and regulations.

Data sourcing, technology, integration and process issues, bias in decision-making algorithms, concerns over intellectual property, reputational implications if use becomes controversial, system security concerns.

The rapid evolution and increased availability of artificial intelligence may intensify cybersecurity risks by making targeted attacks more convincing and cybersecurity incidents more difficult to detect, contain, and mitigate.

limitations: No quantified AI capex or budget figures appear in the excerpts; investment intensity scored 3 based on named initiatives and stated intent to expand, without dollar amounts or headcount.
AI use cases (generative AI in business processes, automation/robotics/ML in products) are named but no deployment metrics or quantified outcomes are disclosed, capping concreteness at 3.

Risk disclosure is company-specific with named mechanisms (regulatory compliance costs, cybersecurity amplification, competitive disadvantage, IP/bias/reputational risks) but no quantified risk exposure or named governance/oversight bodies, capping depth at 4.
